replies.author: @333AppalachiaEnergetics
replies.text: These hills are enchanted from 🫈 to 🧚‍♀️ to the little and moon eyed peoples. Frequently see plasmoid orbs over table rock and hawks bill and through out Linville gorge and even all around my home in the foothills. The annual powwow in Cherokee is open to all every 4th of July weekend. The energy is incredible. The vortices of western NC and east TN are a gentle and feminine energy. Then there are the fireflies ... the synchronous and the blue ghosts put on quite the show in late spring. Usually see a bear or 2 when venturing around the forests. 🧸🍄🧸

comments.author: @eltamarindo
comments.text: Regarding mountain destruction by coal mining. The ancient prevailing winds hit the mountains from specific directions resulting in erosion in certain directions and the formation of peat and eventually coal only in certain parts of the erosion area of these mountains. Thankfully the highest parts of the mountain range are largely unaffected by this blight.

comments.author: @RebeccaDenhoff
comments.text: I live here on a mountain farm.   The wildlife,  plantlife and the  scenery surrounds me 360.  I never go anywhere,  no need.  Life is old here, older than the trees (per John Denver's Take me home).  I have a 500 year old oak tree, a young sprout when the colonies were first established.   It is still healthy, but massive  standing like a mastiff in the river bottom providing shade, food  and protection to wildlife and shade loving plants.  No lights means the Milky Way is visible on clear nites.  The smell is delicious especially after rain.  It's the only place I ever wanted to be.

replies.author: @esotericbrain2689
replies.text: ​@arthurwright255  lmfao you totally missed the whole point didn't you? Many of us have no want or need for any of that slop. We have no wish to have anything to do with your big cities. Amazing that you completely missed the entire pot lmfao 🤣😂

replies.author: @RebeccaDenhoff
replies.text:
​@dannyphantomgirl101
Southwestern Virginia Blue Ridge.

replies.author: @RebeccaDenhoff
replies.text:
​@arthurwright255
People who choose to live in areas such as I do usually do so to get away from society and it's busyness.   I have a few dear friends locally, my animals, nature, farming.  I do not seek anything else, it is all here.  People who move to such places who crave lots of social connections and activities usually leave.  Most here are the same as myself,  they have family or a few good friends.  We are intrinsically different from people who want people around most of the time.  We are the opposite.

replies.author: @arthurwright255
replies.text: One of the biggest issues with living in such an area is less or no access to generous social initiatives like you would in big cities like NYC. With the efforts big cities are making to give residences access to healthcare and free groceries. The cities and their mayors provide more social initiatives and access than small towns do.

replies.author: @creamcheness1115
replies.text: You’re living my dream. I just can’t get out there.

replies.author: @333AppalachiaEnergetics
replies.text: @dannyphantomgirl101 I can see the Milky Way from my home in the foothills of western NC. In the higher elevations it is even more spectacular 💛  🌌 . just last month I went into the forest  to watch the synchronous and blue ghost fireflies. When I came out of the forest the sky was crystal clear.  It was a new moon so the stars had no competition and were stunning. Could vaguely see Andromeda galaxy. Spruce Pine NC has international dark sky designation and a nice observatory. Also, waterrock knob directly off the Blue Ridge Parkway is phenomenal for astrophotographers. All Worth visiting when in the region.

comments.author: @BlueRidgeCritter
comments.text: As a geologist who lives in the Appalachians of Virginia, I usually find shows like this to be a little cringy at times when they get into the geologic history. This one was not bad. Not bad at all. The mountain range before the Appalachians, what we called the ancient Appalachians, were left out, and it's important because that formed the basis of what the current Appalachians were formed out of and kind of guided how they were oriented.. but doesn't really matter, this was pretty good I enjoyed it.

replies.author: @andrewwomble2722
replies.text: I live around the Sauratown Mountains in NC and the geological history of those fascinate because when you climb to the top, not too far away, you see a wall of mountains rise up from the valley which are the Appalachians. From my understanding, the Sauratown mountains are not part of the Appalachians and maybe much older though I'm no Geologist.
